speaker: interviewer
text: It's the 5th of November, 1995, and we are in Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: At Chateau de Morelles--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll wait. We'll just wait for this.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little bit more about Chateau de Morelles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Chateau de Morelles-- having a lot of children. So it was very strictly disciplined, you know? And, again, we were assigned to do various tasks. So one week we were assigned to do the-- in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another time, we were assigned, as I said, before cleaning up the place. What else? We were-- I remember once I was-- I was put on duty for-- in the kitchen. And because we had-- bread was very short, we had a machine which used to do a slice of bread a certain-- it was set up for a certain thickness. So I remember that I made it a bit bigger. [LAUGHS] Because we wanted-- we were-- we were very-- we were always hungry. Meat was very rare. Although, it was a kosher home. But whenever we had meat, maybe once a week or once a month, I don't remember. And I can remember that I was punished for opening up [LAUGHS] the slice. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was an incident.

speaker: interviewee
text: And - And- also, if-- on many occasions, you know, we was-- if we were sick, I remember once I was sick. And-- and the doctor-- we had a doctor in the house. He said to me, OK. Go upstairs. Go into your room, into the-- it was a dormitory. We were 15 to 20 in a dormitory, boys separately, and the girls separately, of course. And I stayed there the whole day. And I was starving. I didn't know-- no one came up to see me and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I decided to get dressed, and I went downstairs at dinnertime. So the doctor said to me, you're feeling better now? You see, that's how we were-- we were taught. So we were scared to say to the doctor that we were sick for fear that, you know, maybe you weren't telling the truth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, of course, in the-- in wintertime, we used to, we always wore-- in those days, kids never wore long- long pants. So we used to have chilblain in the back here when we used to walk in the snow and so on. And also, you know, at night time, the dormitory windows used to open up that were French windows. The snow used to fall in. And even though we were cold, that was part of the upbringing. Now, we had also bathrooms, you know, showers. Once a week-- once a week, they'd put on the hot water. The rest of the time, we were told to wash ourself in cold water. And before we went out of the bathroom, they used to check the back of ours ears, on our knee, everywhere on our foot, that we washed ourself properly. You know, I must say that, for that, cleanliness was very important. Well, they had to. Otherwise there'd be, people, you know, would be sick and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So looking back, that was a good idea. Because even today, I've learned, I've even taught my kids to be-- [LAUGHS] to be extremely tidy and that sort of thing. Because they used to check. In fact, once we were having breakfast, they used to come and put their hands in our bed. And if the bed was still warm, they'd make it undone and tell us, go upstairs. And next time, make sure that your bed is aerated. And on the corner of the bed, it had to be perfectly square, you know? The corners had to be done properly. You see, we didn't have anybody doing our-- our cleanliness. We had to do it ourself. So we learned. This is how we learned discipline, you know? Despite the discipline, was there any sort of warmth? You didn't have parents. You just--

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't-- I don't think that we looked for that. Because we had our friends. We had our clique. You know, and we stuck together. And we share things together as much as we can, you know? And-- and I can remember, you know, when we used to, at night time, we used to-- to go to the toilet, we had to go downstairs. And I-- I was somehow starving. So I went and raided the pantry. [LAUGHS] And of course, they caught me. They- they set a trap the following night. [LAUGHS] They caught me with somebody else. So I was punished. I was put in the cellar.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the cellar, it's-- you know, bats used to fly around. And in the dark, I got used to it. And I found there was some carrots and apples, you know, in shelves. So we decided to eat there. [LAUGHTER] So-- but this is the mischief we used to go-- we used to-- we used to do. You know? But, looking back, you know, it was-- it was good. But at the same time, of course, we didn't have time to think about our parents. Because we were too many of us who were-- who were in the same situation, you know? And when you went to and from the school every day, was there any nervousness that you would be found out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had to be very careful, of course, that, you know, the German wouldn't notice us so much, that we were Jews. And every now and again, if we see apple in-- in the-- you see a big country. So there was a lot of apple trees and pear trees.

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to pinch them on the way, you know? And the farmers used to wait for us. And sometimes they used to shoot-- what do you call-- pellet, a salt pellet. And if you- if you'd get them into your-- into your skin, it did hurt. So we had to be very careful that they didn't see us. But that was-- we would try to survive, of course, you know? And-- [BACKROUND NOISE] And the teachers at school? Did they differentiate between you  and the local children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I can remember. Because, you see, in France it-- the schools are very strict. Of course, you know, you're not allowed to be taught religion at school. That's a Napoleonic law.

speaker: interviewee
text: So no religion was allowed. And although, we were strict-- for instance, in France, you don't go to school on Thursday. But you must go on Saturday. Saturday, public schools are open. And so, because we were not allowed to write on Shabbat, so the teacher had - had made on the date was-- recitation, is it? Or, resuscitation? Recitations. We used to learn about Moliere, La Fontaine, and all that sort of thing, you know, by heart. [BACKROUND NOISE ]

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- so they didn't made us-- they didn't have any, what you call, exams, so that we'd miss out. So I must say, for this they were quite fair. You know, they knew that we were religious, and we were not allowed to write. So it was strictly lectures date on the-- on the Shabbat. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was really not very much mixing between your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a little bit, but not very much. Not very much. And, as I said before, there was a little bit of anti-Semitism. Yes. It was. But, of course, you know, kids will be kids, you know? [BACKROUND NOISE] And there's nothing we could do about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of excursions did you go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to go on-- on-- on-- we used to take, for instance, overnight tents. And we used to go camping on the top of things, like Scouts, virtually. And that we always looked forward to see that. And we'd go and visit a-- a museum if there was one around there. We used to, on long, we used to go sometime for three or four days at a time. We used to do our own cooking on the fire, you know, that type of things. That they were the best time then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And 50 of you would go, the people in your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Sometimes more, of course. If it's during the holidays, we used to go more. And then, of course, there was more teachers coming, as well. Would probably be about 50, 60 then. You know, it was good fun. Sleep in sleeping bags or whatever we had in summer time.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there was always an element of caution?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the time, yes. Most of the time. Because we were told by our teacher, the people who looked after us, to be very careful, of course. And definitely not, like I said, to speak Yiddish. That was forbidden you see. So-- and that's how it went on like this, till 19-- till April '43. Then-- would you like me to continue on that?

speaker: interviewee
text: So, apparently, the police came and told the headmaster, please clear the place. Because they're coming to pick you up tomorrow. So we split overnight, virtually.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever we could carry, we took with us. And we tried to-- I don't know where everybody went. But one of-- our group of about 30, I would imagine, went to the Chateau de [INAUDIBLE], up in the mountain.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We took a train. I would say it would be at least two to three hours. And we went to the Chateau de [INAUDIBLE]. And it was run by Rabbi Schneerson. That was extremely religious there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were taught Gemora, Rashi, [INAUDIBLE], from sunset [LAUGHS] to sunset. And we stayed there for-- we also used to go on excursion and walk and so on up in the mountain. And then it got-- we stayed there till September. That was how long altogether?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from April to September. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no secular schooling at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't go to secular school. We stayed definitely-- we-- we had our schooling in the home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was a very old chateau as well. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we used to have chicken. And we used to have, what do you call, ducks, geese, and so on. It was really very nice, our own little-- little farm. And all we did was-- quite serious, actually, it was. Because we-- we were taught virtually Gemora, Rashi, and all that sort of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that home, I would imagine there would have been about maybe 100. But some of them were very much older than us, the ones who were there before us. And I remember, we were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and around about September, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was an alert that we're to clear again. So we decided-- Rabbi Schneerson had organized that we go to Switzerland. Excuse me. And.. [DRINKS] To go to Switzerland was not an easy thing, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Pardon me. May we just talk a little bit more about Chateau de [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- I've got very little recollection there, except that, you know, again, we were given task to do. And I can remember I was-- on one other occasion, I was peeling some peas in the pod. And the Rebbetzin Schneerson said to me, we had those geese coming in and tried to eat them.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, chase them up quick. So I chased them up, and one of them fell into a ditch or something like that. And he had a broken-- broken wing. So they set the following day, trying to find, is it kosher or is it not kosher?

speaker: interviewee
text: You wouldn't believe. There, we are starving. They decide it's treif. We're not allowed to eat it, because it's got a broken wing. So we had to give it away. [LAUGHS] And I was not too popular for that. That's the only type of small incident that I can remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your teachers go with you to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had Miriam, and then [? Dybnis ?] with us. And they followed us all along. And as I said, I had not too-- too much recollection there. Because already, it got-- it's got a-- it was a little bit scary. Because, you know, we were worried, looking around, you know, whether the Germans were looking for us. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of people around you, the local people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so much, no. No. In the country, no. So what happened was this. That there was an alert. And we had to get out again within 24 hours. So we assembled. And I can remember, I had very little to take again. And as I left-- as we left the dormitory, I saw a tefillin. I was 10 years old then. I saw a tefillin and a tallis on the mantel. And I decided to take them with me in the little bag that I had. Now, the only-- what worried me, too, is my sister had gone down to-- had- had gone to town with a group of friends. And I-- I was-- I didn't want to leave, because I didn't want to leave my sister behind. And I started to cry and, you know, and-- look, you'll see her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we decided to walk to the station, which was 2 kilometers away, again, or 4 kilometers. And on the way, my sister was just walking with the others. So she didn't even have the time to go back to the-- to our home to pick up her goods. She had to follow us and came with nothing. Everything was left there. And you children were carrying--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not suitcase. We couldn't carry suitcase, too heavy for us. We just had whatever we had on our-- just a little bag. That's all we had. And we-- we took-- we went to Limoges. And I remember that evening, we arrived at the station. And they put us into upstairs in the station in Limoges. And about 3 o'clock in the morning, we heard noise. So we looked out outside the window. And we saw the German assembling Jews from their home and marching them to a camp. And we watched them from the-- from the top floor, what they were doing. The next day, we took a train to the border. Somehow, Rabbi Schneerson had paid some non-Jewish people to-- to take us through forests, through field, you know, the way to the-- to the-- to the border. And it took us quite a while to get through. In fact, if you know when--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us the journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We took a train. When we arrived at Annemasse, Annemasse is on the border of Swiss and France. It's a French town on the Swiss border as well. And we wanted to get-- we got off the train. And they said, quickly, get back on the train. Because the Germans have-- just happened to be around for that-- for that time in that area, so you can't go through the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you look like Jews, traveling with the Rabbi and the Rebbetzin?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the Rabbi and the Rebbetzin stayed. They stayed in Chateau de [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: With any--

speaker: interviewee
text: With the others. With the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, whoever was left. And the ones that was from [INAUDIBLE] came with us. We had split into groups of 10, 12, I remember. And it took us quite a while to get to-- you know, we took the train. We waited for a week. Somehow we-- I-- I can't remember. We had to go back. And we had to wait for the German to clear that area. So, finally, we got to Annemasse. And when we-- we got off the train--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. Do you remember where you stayed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't. We stayed in-- in-- in what you call farms, on straw, you know, in-in barns and things like that. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you think that the farmers knew who they were looking after or taking in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they must have been. I think we were with the passeur. Passeur is those people who showed us the way. They were non-Jews. They had trucks, which was covered. And they-- they-- we slept here and there, you know, wherever they-- it was safe. Finally--

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of places did you sleep in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in barns, in farms. And the farmers used to give us something to eat. And then we-- we went into those trucks. and they-- through forests. It was-- I remember the day it was pouring rain. We were cold, hungry. And then the truck stopped. And they said, that's as far as you can-- that's as far as we can go. You'll have to walk the rest of the way.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we walked through-- we went across river-- not rivers, but creeks and, you know, through forests and so on. And again, till we reach-- Sorry. What time of the year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in September. It's- It was beginning to be quite cold. So there was no-- and then, when we reached the-- the border, I remember, we were given the special-- what do they call those? To cut the barbed wires?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] well, yeah, is it [NON-ENGLISH]? And we cut them to go across. And we were there for-- we walked for about 50 meters. Suddenly, out of the tree, you got the Swiss Guard jump down. We got the shock of our life, because the Swiss has exactly the same uniform as a German. And the only difference is the Swiss, their buttons is a cross. And the German is an eagle. But you see, when you're in the forest, you can't look like that. And we thought that these people who took us across the border took us to Germany. Well, my sister yelled out, [BACKROUND NOISE] run, run, run. So my brother and I started to run into Switzerland, which we thought it was Switzerland. Meantime, you know, the others were-- they-- the Swiss Guard had-- had arrested them, virtually. And my brother and I were left alone in the forest. And after about 10 minutes, I said to my brother, let's go back and see what's happened. So we walked through the trees, and so on. And we saw everybody lying down on the ground. And the Swiss Guard made a sign. Please lie down. Excuse me. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we watched. And 50 meters away, a German patrol with dogs just passed by. So we just made it. Amongst our groups, we-- all of us made it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some groups didn't make it. We never found out what's happened. One of my close friend, he disappeared. He was shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edmond, Edmond Belk, his name was. And his-- his brother is still alive in Paris today.

speaker: interviewer
text: His brother's name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. Can't remember. It's so many years ago. And-- excuse me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we stayed there for a good hour lying on the ground, raining. And we finally found out, there's the Swiss-- what the Swiss used to do on the French border, they used to put-- you know, in Switzerland they-- in certain part of Switzerland, they speak German, Switzerdeutsch.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they put the-- the German Swiss on the French border as presumed to stop people going into-- coming into the country. Because that would bamboozle them, which it did on many occasion. Because when they saw the uniform, they were speaking Swiss-German. So they went back. And when they went back, they got caught. You see? So we made-- we walked for about two hours. Yet, they - they protected you when you came.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, once we're in Switzerland, you know, I don't remember whether they were pushing back groups. But when they-- I suppose when they saw-- people saw the Swiss dressed up like Germans, that was the uniform. They got scared, so they went back. And that's when they were caught.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we walked for about two hours. And we reached a-- a Swiss-- what you call-- where all the soldiers are? [NON-ENGLISH] they call it in French. I don't know in English. Barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barracks, yeah. And there they put us on a [BACKROUND NOISE] bus and took us to a transit camp, a transit camp in Geneva.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the sort of feelings that-- can you remember how you felt?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- well, it was very scary. It was very, very scary. I can't remember the exact feeling of it, but we were not too happy. Because, [BACKROUND NOISE] you know, the older-- the older boys and girls, naturally, were crying. And we didn't know what's going to happen to us, and so on. And we reached a transit camp in Geneva. It was a school. It-- the school was called [INAUDIBLE]. And that was guarded day and night. We were not allowed to go out.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was guarded by Swiss Guards. And if we needed something to buy, once a week, somebody was in charge of the camp. They used to go and get it for us. You see? So-- and as we arrived there, they decided to get us, each of us, to where do you come from? They-- to interview us. They wanted to find out-- we found out afterwards the reason why.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister always used to say to me, you don't know. You-- you-- I don't want you to open your mouth. Otherwise, you're going to [INAUDIBLE] from me. And as small as we were, they didn't take the three of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us all separately. So they took me. I was the first to go in, and the youngest one of the three of us. They asked me, where did you come from? I said, I don't know. I was-- you know. She was always suspicious, my sister. So much so, she was quite right. Because we found out that the person who was interviewing us was-- was somebody who used to gather addresses where you're from. And they used to say, have you still got parents where you lived, or family?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they used to write down everything. And we found out they were working for the-- for the German unbeknown to the Swiss people. They followed, and this-- this couple, at the end of the week, at the end of the week were allowed to go out shopping for all of us. And they managed to compile a whole list of the people. Because people used to come daily from - from France, or whatever. And they'd compiled a list. And they'd put it into a tin in the forest outside Geneva. And it was an espionage. And this is how they used to pick up, say, parents that you had left over there, that you had left in France, or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they arrested these people. And we moved away from the camp. They put us into another camp. Can you tell us about the-- the interview that you had with these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they ask us, actually, where are you from. They asked us, you know, are your parents still living there? Or, do you have an auntie? Do you have cousins? Where do they live? They wanted to know everything about. And they did the same thing with my brother. And, of course, my sister, you know, she was older than us. She was six years older than me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she knew exactly what to say. She was 16 years old. And yet, she didn't trust anybody. She's a most amazing woman she is.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, she had suspicious. I mean, you know, she didn't have a-- a youth life. She was born, and immediately she was an adult, because she looked after us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think it made you grow up very quickly, also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, as well. Yes. Well, we missed our youth, virtually. Because by the time we were, let's say, 15, 16, we had lived like somebody who was 25 with all what happened to us. I think it's normal. No?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened once you got to this place where you were being questioned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there we-- it was not a pleasant place. We used to sleep in-- we used to sleep on the-- in-- it was a school, so we slept in-- in the classroom. The classroom was divided into-- into sections, you know, like 2 meters. There was a board and the straw. We used to sleep on straw on the floor. We were given blankets. And we stayed there, I think, for about three or four weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had the school been closed? It wasn't being used as a school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not being used as a school, no. And then, that-- that school became so crowded, they decide to move the old-- the ones who arrived earlier, to another school. The other school was called-- it was called a [INAUDIBLE] also in Geneva, not very far from-- from where that was. And, of course, it had a big playground, so it was much better. But, again, we slept on the- on the floor, on the board, and in the-- in the classrooms. And I can remember in 1943, it was Rosh Hashanah arrived. And we were trying to get a-- story was like that. We were trying to get--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. Can we keep this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah... Ok

speaker: interviewer
text: --for the next tape? Because I think we're just coming to the end of this one.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape two of the interview with Leon Wise. It's the 5th of November, 1995, and we are in Sydney, Australia.
